Marriage — Formation Requirements.rule: A valid marriage requires legal capacity, mutual consent, and compliance with statutory formalities, unless an exception applies.
Marriage — Formation Requirements.application: Because both parties had capacity and consented in compliance with formalities, the marriage is valid.

Marriage — Common Law Marriage.rule: Where recognized, a common law marriage requires capacity, present intent to be married, cohabitation, and holding out as married.
Marriage — Common Law Marriage.application: Because the parties cohabited and held themselves out as married, a common law marriage exists.

Marriage — Void and Voidable Marriages.rule: Void marriages are invalid from inception, while voidable marriages are valid unless annulled due to defects such as fraud, duress, incapacity, or age.
Marriage — Void and Voidable Marriages.application: Because the defect affected consent, the marriage is voidable and subject to annulment.

Putative Spouse Doctrine.rule: A putative spouse is entitled to marital protections when a party has a good faith belief in the validity of the marriage.
Putative Spouse Doctrine.application: Because the spouse believed in good faith that the marriage was valid, putative spouse protections apply.

Annulment.rule: Annulment declares a marriage invalid from the beginning based on a defect existing at the time of formation.
Annulment.application: Because fraud existed at formation, annulment is appropriate.

Premarital and Separation Agreements.rule: Premarital and separation agreements are enforceable if voluntary, supported by full disclosure, and not unconscionable; courts review child-related provisions.
Premarital and Separation Agreements.application: Because the agreement lacked disclosure, it is unenforceable.

Divorce — Grounds and Jurisdiction.rule: Divorce may be granted on fault or no-fault grounds, and jurisdiction exists where at least one spouse is domiciled in the state.
Divorce — Grounds and Jurisdiction.application: Because one spouse was domiciled in the state and irreconcilable differences existed, the court had jurisdiction to grant divorce.

Property Division — Marital vs Separate.rule: Marital property includes assets acquired during marriage, while separate property includes premarital assets, gifts, and inheritances unless transmuted.
Property Division — Marital vs Separate.application: Because the asset was acquired before marriage and not transmuted, it remains separate.

Property Division — Equitable Distributi.rule: Equitable distribution divides marital property fairly but not necessarily equally, while community property states divide marital property equally.
Property Division — Equitable Distributi.application: Because the state applies equitable distribution, the court divided property based on fairness.

Spousal Support (Alimony).rule: Spousal support may be awarded and modified based on factors including need, ability to pay, duration of marriage, and a substantial change in circumstances.
Spousal Support (Alimony).application: Because the marriage was long-term and earning capacity was unequal, spousal support was appropriate.

Child Custody — Best Interests and Types.rule: Child custody determinations are based on the best interests of the child and may include legal custody, physical custody, or joint custody.
Child Custody — Best Interests and Types.application: Because stability favored one parent, custody was awarded accordingly.

Child Custody — Modification and Relocat.rule: Custody orders may be modified upon a substantial change in circumstances, and relocation requires good faith and consistency with the child’s best interests.
Child Custody — Modification and Relocat.application: Because the child’s environment changed significantly, modification was justified.

Child Custody Jurisdiction (UCCJEA).rule: Under the UCCJEA, initial custody jurisdiction lies in the child’s home state where the child lived for six months prior to filing.
Child Custody Jurisdiction (UCCJEA).application: Because the child lived in the state for six months, it is the home state.

Child Support — Obligation and Modificat.rule: Parents have a non-waivable duty to support their children, and support may be modified upon a substantial change in circumstances.
Child Support — Obligation and Modificat.application: Because income changed substantially, child support was modified.

Child Support Jurisdiction (UIFSA).rule: Under UIFSA, the issuing state retains continuing exclusive jurisdiction over child support orders unless all parties leave the state.
Child Support Jurisdiction (UIFSA).application: Because one parent still resided in the issuing state, jurisdiction remained.

Parentage (Paternity).rule: Paternity may be established through marriage, voluntary acknowledgment, adjudication, or genetic testing, with a marital presumption for children born during marriage.
Parentage (Paternity).application: Because the child was born during marriage, the husband is presumed to be the father.

Adoption.rule: Adoption requires consent of biological parents unless parental rights have been terminated.
Adoption.application: Because parental rights were terminated, adoption was permitted.

Termination of Parental Rights.rule: Parental rights may be terminated upon clear and convincing evidence of abuse, neglect, abandonment, or unfitness.
Termination of Parental Rights.application: Because chronic abuse was proven, termination was appropriate.

Domestic Violence — Protective Orders.rule: Courts may issue protective orders to prevent domestic violence, including ex parte orders based on affidavits of abuse or threats.
Domestic Violence — Protective Orders.application: Because abuse was credibly alleged, a protective order was issued.
